content.full_text:
Farn Polrrrc,q,l PRACTTcEs CouprrssroN
428 J Street e Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
March 9,2007
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the revolving door
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act').r This letter is based on the facts
presented in your request. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when issuing
advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Our advice is applicable only to the
extent that the facts provided to us are correct, and all material fatts have been provided.
Because your questions are general in nafure and do not refer to any specific appearance
before or communication with a state administrative agency, inctuding your former
agency employer, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.'
QUESTION
Do the post-governmental employment provisions of the Act prohibit you from
appearing before or communicatingwith the California State Lands Commission (the
"CSLC") regarding projects that you were involved with while employed by the CSLC?
CONCLUSION
For your pulposes the one-yearbanhas expired, and it will no longer restrict your
appearances before or communications with the CSLC. However, the permanent ban will
apply to anyproceeding related to an Environmental Impact Report 1..8IR,,; for an
application to lease state lands submitted while you were a GSLC employee.
I Govemment Code sections 8 I 000-9 1 014. Commission regulations appear at tille 2,sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.j
Stephen L. Jenkins
Director of Governmental Services
Michael Brandman Associates
11060 White Rock Road, Suite 150
Rancho Cordova, California 95670
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-07-017
Dear Mr. Jenkins
@


File No. I-07-017
Page No. 2
FACTS
You are a former designated employee of the CSLC and served in a management
position. You resigned from this position on January 2,2006- your role with thtCSLC
was to provide analysis of applications to lease state lands to ensure compliance with the
Califomia Environmental Quality Act (the "CEQA'). You also made r"io--"rdations
on mitigation measures that would become conditions of approval for a lease should an
application be approved by the CSLC. Since leaving the CSLC, you have worked as a
consultant with Michael Brandman Associates.
In telephone conversations on February 23,20a7, and March 2,2007,you
explained that when an applicant applies to lease state lands the CSLC must determine
what level of review is necessary under the CEQA. Depending on the level of review,
the CSLC mayneed to complete an EIR. The CSLC often hires consultants to complete
the report. Ultimately, you made the final determination of the necessary level of C^EeA
review any one project would require. When an application was submitted, you were
also responsible for assigning and supervising a staffmember who would u"i u, a project
manager for the proposed project. This staffmember would begrn to collect information
relevant to the project, including information related to any environmental issues that
would be used to determine the necessary level of cEeA review.
As a consultant for Michael Brandman Associates, you would like to contract
with the CSLC to prepare EIRs related to applications to lease state lands. you further
explained that you were particularly concemed with whether the permanent ban prohibits
you from contracting with the CSLC to perform EIRs related to applications to lease state
lands that you previously worked on as an employee ofthe csLC.
ANALYSIS
As we discussed in our prior letter to you, Jenkins Advice Letter, No. 4-06-050,
public officials who leave state service are subject to two types ofpost-governmental
.-919ry*tprovisions under the Act, colloquially known as the ,i"rolr.irg door,,
prohibitions.' we discuss your question under each of these provisions.
o One'Year Ban: The "one-yearban" prohibits a state anployee from appearing
before or corlmunicating with, for compensation, his orher for-o ug*iy for ttre
3 In addition, section 87407 prohibits certain state and local officials from makjng, participating in
making, or using their official position to influence decisions affecting persons with whom-they are
negotiating employment, or have any arrangement concerning employment. (Section 87407;rlguhtion
18747.)


File No. I-07-017
Page No. 3
purpose of influencing certain adminiskative or legislative action or influencing
certain proceedings. (see section g7 406; regulation I g7 46 J )4
The one year ban applies only to appearances or cofilmunications made within 12
morrths of leaving state office or employment and made before or with an agency the
official worked for or represented (or an agency under the budgetary or upfrirrtir"
control of the agency the official worked for or represented) during the li months before
leaving state office or employment. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(2) eIrrdOX6).) Based on t]le
facts you have provided, you left yourposition with the CSLC over 14 months ago, on
January 2,2006. Accordingly,the one-yearban has expired, and your appearances
before or conrmunications with the CSLC are restrictedonly if they fall under the
permanent ban as described below.
o Permanent Ran: The "permanent ban"prohibits a former state employee from
"switching sides" and participating, for compensation, in any specific proceeding
involving the State of California or assisting others in the prlceeding if the
proceeding is one in which the former state employee participated wlile
employed by the state (see sections 87401-87402; regglatiorrl8T4l.l).
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any judicial, quasi-judicial, or
other proceeding in which you participated while you served as a state aimini"strative
official. "'Judicial, quasi-judicial or otherproceeding, means anyproceeding,
application, request for a ruling or other determination, contract, "iui-, "o.rtiorrory,
investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or otherparticularmatter involving a ,pe"ifi"
party or parties in any court or state administrative agency . . -.,, (Section s'z+ob1c;.;
Additionally, an official is considered to have "participated" in aproceeding if he or she
took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially througtrdecision,-approval,
disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantiaibasis,
investigation, or use of confidential information . . ..,, (section g7400(d).)
We point out that the permanent ban applies only to proceedings you participated
in as a state ernployee. If the proposal to prepare the EIR involves aiflerent purti".,
different subject matter, or different factual or legal issues from those considered in
previous proceedings the proceedings may be considered 'hew proceedings,, to which the
permanent ban would not apply.
To begin our analysis, from all indications you have permanantly left state
service, and, you are being compensated for participating in the proceeding in
representation of Michael Brandman Associates. Moreovo, -ukirrg a profosal to
prepare an EIR is a particular matter involving a specific party or parties before a state
administrative agency and involves a contract between the party or parties and the state
a An appearance or communication include, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or in
person' corresponding in writing or by electronic transmission, attending a meeting, *dd"li*.i.rg o,
sending any communication. (Regulation I 87 46.2.)


File No. I-07-017
Page No. 4
administrative agency. Thus, any proceeding related to the EIR will comprise a judicial,
quasi-judicial, or other proceeding to which the permanent ban will apply if you
previously participated in the proceeding. (See Walter Adice Letter, i..fo. l-OO-O7g and
Cooper Advice Letter, No. A-99-094.)
Regulation l874l.l further defines "previous participation" as participating
"personally and substantially by making, participating in making or in^fluen.ing of u
govemmental decision, as defined in [regulations] 18702 .l-18702.4. . ... Und& these
provisions, a public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contrictual
agreement on behalf ofhis or her agurcy. (Regulationl8702.L) A public official
"pxticipates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the auihority of his or her
position and without significant intervening substantive review, the official negotiates,
advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use h]s or her official
position to influence a decision if for the purpose of influencirrg, ,h" official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3.)
As noted, the permanent ban only restricts you from participating in those
proceedings in which you have previouslyparticipated. Accordingly, fo-rthose
applications submitted after you had left the SLC and in which yo" irua no prior
involvement, thepermanent ban would not prohibit you from contracting *ith th" CSLC
to perform EIRs related to the applications.
However, we must separately consider those applications submitted after you had
left the CSLC if you had discussed an application with an applicant prior to leaving the
CSLC. As discussed above, the permanent ban will only applyif you had previouJly
participated personally and substantially by making, participating in making, or
influencing a govemmental decision. Discussions with the applicant prior io the
application to lease state lands that occurred prior to any determinations related to the
EIR would not constitute making, participating in making or influencing governmental
decisions related to the EIR. Accordingly, the perman"rt U* would not prohibit you
from contracting with the CSLC to prepare an environmental impact repo; so long as the
application to lease state lands was submitted after you had left the csic.
In terms of applications filed while you were an ernployee of the CSLC, you have
provided that all applications had to be reviewed to determine the necessary level of
review, as required under the CEQA, and that you would assign the applications to staff
members to serve as project managers. Under your superrrision, the staffmemUers would
collect information relevant to the application including information related to any
environmental issues. This information was subsequentlyused to determine the
appropriate level of CEQA review.


File No. I-07-017
Page No. 5
Under regulation 18741.1, a "superyisor is deemed to have participated in any
proceeding that was 'pending before' . . . the official's agency and that was under his or
her'superwisory authority' . . .." A proceeding is under a supervisor,s ..supervisory
authority" if the supervisor:
"(A) Has duties that include primary responsibility within the agency for
directing the operation or function of the program where *reproceejing is
initiated or conducted; or
"(B) Has direct supervision of the p€rson performing the investigation,
review, or other action involved in the proceeding including, but not
Iimited to, assigning the matter for which the required conduct is taken; or
"(c) Reviews, discusses, or authorizes any action in the proceeding; or
"(D) Has any contact with any of the participants in the proceeding
regarding the subject of the proceeding.,,
The determination of the appropriate level of CEQA review is a decision in
respects to a proceeding and is not a purely ministerial decision. Thus, any proceeding
related to an EIR, for an application filed while you were a CSLC employee, ** p"ndirrg
before your agency. (Regulation 18438.2(bX3).) Moreover, as you assigned the staff
member to act as the project manager and had direct supervisionover th[ staffmernber,
anyproceeding related to an EIR for an application filed while you were a CSLC
employee, was under your supervisory authority. (Regulation itl+t.t1u1(4XB).)
We conclude that you have previously participated in any proceedings related to
EIRs for applications filed while you were a CSLC ernployee and that the pJrmanent ban
prohibits you from appearing before or communicatingwith any officer oiemployee of
the csLC for the purpose of influencing any of these proceedings
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
y: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division
BGLjgl

Stephen L. Jenkins
Director of Governmental Services

Our Dear Mr. Jenkins
